content.full_text:
Farn Por-rrrcer- PRACTrcss C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
Apri|19,2002
Tony Daysog, Councilmember
City of Alameda
146 Santa Clara Avenue
Alameda, CA 94501
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-087
Dear Mr. Daysog:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").\
QUESTION
Is it a conflict of interest under the Act for you to simultaneously serve on the
Alameda City Council and also be employed by a private economic development
consulting company (ADE), which has entered into a contract to provide services to
another company (Catellus Development Corporation) in connection with a development
agreement with the city?
CONCLUSION
The Act does not prohibit a public official from sirnultaneously serving as a
public official and being employed privately. Rather, the Act prohibits public officials
from making, participating in making or using their positions to influence a governmental
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their
financial interests.
1 Government Code $$ 81000 - 91014. Commissionregulations appear at Title 2, $S 18i09-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
g


File No. A-02-087
Page No. 2
FACTS
In the winter of 2002, the City ofAlameda staff requesledthat Catellus
Development Corporation update the "economic development strategic plan,"
specifically the retail market component of that plan. Catellus is developing a2l5-acre
site, which includes acreage for retail. In turn, Catellus called one of the managing
principals at ADE during the week of March 18,2002, to ask if it could hire ADE to
update that retail market analysis. You are employed at ADE as a city planner. You do
not have an ownership interest in the company. Further, you will not work on the
Catellus project for ADE.
ANALYSIS
A. The ADB/Catellus Contract
The Act does not prohibit a public official from being employed outside of
government. Instead, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. ($ 87100.) In order to determine
whether the prohibition in $ 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides
the following eight-step analysis.
Steps One and Two: Are you a'opublic official" and will you be making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
As a city council member in Alameda, you are a "member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency" and, as such, you are a "public offrcial"
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (g 82048; Reg. 18701(a) ) A
public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, actingwithin the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or
her agency to any course ofaction, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalfof
his or her agency. (R"e. 18702.1 ) A public official "participates in making a
governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and
without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision.
(Reg. 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision i{, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Reg. 18702.3.)
Your primary question involves the relationship between your private and public
employer, not the making, participating in making, or the influencing of governmental
decisions. To the extent that there is no specific decision, the Act has nothing to say on
the arrangement. Of course, where there is a decision involving your employer, you may
still be obligated to disqualify yourself.


File No. A-02-087
Page No. 3
We would caution that our advice is limited to the Political Reform Act, and other
provisions of the law may apply (such as Government Code $1090 which governs the
propriety of certain contractual relationships rather than decisionmaking). The Attorney
General's office is responsible for the implementation of Government Code $ 1090.
B. Future Decision on the Catellus Development Proposal
While we cannot advise on every possible future decision that comes before you
as a city council member, we can provide a general analysis that you must use in
consideration of potential future conflicts of interest.
Step Three: Do you have economic interests?
Under $ 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic interests that
may result in a conflict of interest for a public official. Your question concerns your
employment with and income from ADE. Regulation 18703.1 provides:
"A public official has an economic interest in a business
entity if any of the following are true:
"(a)'fl....11
"(b) The public official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management in the
business entity."
Further, regulation I 8703.3(a) provides
"A public official has an economic interest in any person
from whom helshe has received income aggregating five hundred
dollars ($500) within 12 months prior to the time when the relevant
governmental decision is made. For purposes of the Political
Reform Acq a public official's income includes income which has
been promised to the public official but not yet received by him or
her, if he or she has a legally enforceable right to the promised
income."
Thus, ADE is an economic interest under both these provisions. You have
provided no facts to suggest that Catellus is an economic interest.
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
ADE will only be indirectly involved in decisions on the Catellus disposition and
development agreement. Regulation 18704. 1 provides:


File No. A-02-087
Page No. 4
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by
an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."
Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on your economic interest
be material and reasonably foreseeable?
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a materral
financial effect on any of those economic interests. This determination takes two steps.
First, the official must find and apply the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. (Reg. 18700(b)(5) and 18705, et seq.) After finding the
applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Reg. 18700(b)(6).)
Regulation 18705.I sets forth the materiality standards that apply to economrc
interests in business entities. You noted that ADE is a small company with 15
employees. For such a small company, the applicable materiality standards would be in
regulation 18705.1(c)(+) They are:
"[T]he financial effect of a governmental decision on the
business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year
in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more, or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities
of $20,000 or more."


File No. A-02-087
Page No. 5
You must apply this analysis and this materiality threshold on a decision-by-
decision basis.
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions
We have not gone on to analyzelhe latter two steps. Step se\/en is an exception
that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
and step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that these
exceptions may apply.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
W. Wallace
General Counsel
Legal Division
